title: Ticks Are Out in Force: Doctors Warn of a Potentially Severe Season for Lyme Disease
summary: If you're planning to spend time outdoors this spring, listen up: doctors are warning that tick-borne diseases could be especially bad this year. 'If you have a lot of exposures, there will probably be more cases of tick-related infections,' said Dr. Alina Filozov, an infectious disease specialist at Middlesex Hospital in Connecticut. The CDC has issued an early advisory, urging people to protect themselves. The numbers are alarming: weekly rates of ER visits for tick bites are the highest for this time of year since 2017, and that trend is seen across all regions except the south-central U.S. Why the surge? Several factors are at play. Climate change is a key driver — ticks thrive in warm, humid weather, and a mild winter means more ticks survive. Additionally, there have been unusually high numbers of mice in the last two years, providing plenty of hosts for ticks to feed on. In Connecticut, the Agricultural Experiment Station reports residents are submitting an average of 30 ticks per day for testing. The most common tick-borne disease is Lyme disease, which affects an estimated 476,000 people each year. But ticks can also transmit Rocky Mountain spotted fever and alpha-gal syndrome, a red meat allergy. Tiny nymph ticks, which emerge in May, are especially dangerous because they are hard to see and can attach for days, increasing infection risk. Experts recommend practical steps: walk in the middle of trails, wear light-colored clothing treated with permethrin (an insecticide), and use EPA-registered repellents. If you find a tick, remove it immediately. See a doctor only if the tick was attached for days or if you develop a rash or symptoms. Stay vigilant — the outdoors are calling, but so are the ticks.
why_it_matters: As climate change alters tick habitats, teens who enjoy hiking, camping, or gardening face a growing health risk that requires understanding both prevention and the science behind disease transmission.

questions.question: What is the main reason doctors warn this could be a severe tick season?
options: Climate change and mild winters let more ticks survive
There are fewer predators for ticks
questions.correct_answer: Climate change and mild winters let more ticks survive

questions.question: Which tick-borne disease affects an estimated 476,000 people per year?
Why are nymph ticks particularly dangerous?
options: They are tiny and can attach for days without being noticed
questions.correct_answer: They are tiny and can attach for days without being noticed

questions.question: What does the CDC recommend wearing to prevent tick bites?
Which region of the U.S. is not showing an increase in ER visits for tick bites?
What unusual animal population increase contributed to more ticks?

background_read: Lyme disease is caused by bacteria (Borrelia burgdorferi) and spread through the bite of infected blacklegged ticks (also called deer ticks).

Ticks go through four life stages: egg, larva, nymph, and adult. The nymph stage is active in late spring and early summer, and nymphs are tiny (about the size of a poppy seed), making them hard to spot.

Climate change has led to milder winters and longer warm seasons, which allow more ticks to survive and reproduce. Warmer weather also expands the geographic range of ticks into new areas, including parts of Canada.

The CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention) tracks tick‑borne illnesses. Lyme disease is the most common in the U.S., but Rocky Mountain spotted fever can be fatal if untreated. Alpha‑gal syndrome, a delayed allergy to red meat, has also been rising.
In Connecticut, the Agricultural Experiment Station tests ticks submitted by residents. The average of 30 ticks per day submitted indicates high local tick activity.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Warning of severe tick season ahead
└─ expert quote: Dr. Alina Filozov 'If you have a lot of exposures...'
EVIDENCE: CDC numbers show highest ER visits for tick bites since 2017
├─ Climate change: warm, humid weather helps ticks thrive
└─ High mouse population: more hosts for ticks to feed on
- Wear light-colored clothing treated with permethrin
└─ Remove ticks immediately; see doctor if attached days or symptoms appear

perspectives.description: Increased awareness through CDC warnings helps people take precautions, potentially reducing the number of Lyme cases this season.
The surge in ticks reflects a broader environmental problem; many families may not have access to repellents or knowledge about tick removal, leading to unequal health impacts.
The factors behind the tick boom — climate change, mouse cycles — are natural and human‑driven, making future seasons unpredictable without systemic changes.
Better vaccines for Lyme disease are in development, but until then, prevention and early detection remain key. Public health campaigns may need to expand as ticks spread to new regions.
